comments.text: In [Ch00], he proves those results with the constraint that $a_{i+1} -a_i=\{d_1,d_2\}$ instead of $d_1<a_{i+1}-a_i<d_2$. Therefore under the notation defined on this page, $r_2(a,b)>2$ if $b>2a$.

I don't follow this - Theorem 1 of [Ch00] implies in particular that if $b_{n+1}\geq 2b_n$ then there is a sequence $A$ in which $a_{n+1}-a_n\in \{a,b\}$ and $(A+A)\cap B=\emptyset$. We then certainly have $a\leq a_{n+1}-a_n\leq b$, and hence $r_2(a,b)\leq 2$, using the definitions above.

Yes, my issue is actually the other way around. "Chen [Ch00] proved that $r_2(a,b)\leq 2$ for any integers $a<b$ with $b\neq 2a$, and that $r_2(a,2a)\geq 2$ for all integers $a$." Due to how $r_k(a,b)$ is phrased here, for $a_1\leq a<b\leq b_1$, $r_k(a_1,b_1)\leq r_k(a,b)$. So as $r_2(a,b)\leq 2$ for $b<2a$, we must have $r_2(a,b)\leq 2$ for $b=2a$ when $a>1$.

Ah, I see. I still don't think that $r_2(a,b)>2$ if $b>2a$ is right though - I think the simplest fix is just to say that $r_2(a,b)\leq 2$ for any $a<b$ (noting that this follows from e.g. $r_2(a,b)\leq r_2(a,a+1)$ and then using [Ch00] with $b=a+1$.
My first comment was wrong. I agree $r_2(a,b)\leq 2$ if $b>2a$. Also, Theorem 2 of [CH00] shows that $r_2(1,2)$ does not exist.
The question asks about $r\in \mathbb{Z}$, so is $r_k(a,b)$ defined as the smallest integer $r$ that satisfies the condition, or the smallest real $r$?

I guess it makes sense to have it be smallest real $r$ in the quantitative question of $r_k(a,b)$ (although, as already noted, this is going beyond what Erdős himself ever asked about, and this definition only appears in the later work [BHJ97]).
